metadata.title: Car-insurance telematics / surveillance pricing — UBI apps, the covert automaker→LexisNexis/Verisk→insurer data pipeline, FTC v. GM, and Arity's phone-SDK tracking (both sides, dated)
metadata.status: the UBI products, the GM/OnStar covert data sales, the FTC order, the California + Texas AG actions, and the Arity phone-SDK collection are FACT (FTC/AG records, NYT, news of record); the user's two framings ('location-inference price discrimination' and 'no-alternative collective coercion') are GRADED — neither adopted as asserted fact nor dismissed
metadata.frame: The auto/surveillance/insurance nexus. Usage-based insurance (UBI) telematics (Progressive Snapshot, Allstate Drivewise, State Farm, Root) tracks speed/braking/miles/phone-use/time/LOCATION via dongles or always-on phone apps. The bigger story is the COVERT pipeline: automakers (GM OnStar 'Smart Driver'; also Honda/Hyundai/Kia) sold driving + geolocation data to data brokers LexisNexis Risk Solutions + Verisk, who repackaged it into insurance risk scores -> premium hikes WITHOUT consumer consent (NYT, Mar 2024). Enforcement followed: FTC v. GM/OnStar (order finalized Jan 2026), California AG ($12.75M), Texas AG v. Allstate/Arity (Jan 2025). Attaches to GM, FTC, State_AGs, US_Auto_Makers, and the surveillance/ownership-erosion thread.

the_ubi_apps: Usage-based insurance: insurers offer 'discounts' for installing a dongle or always-on phone app that scores driving — Progressive Snapshot, Allstate Drivewise, State Farm Drive Safe & Save, Root (app-first). The apps read phone sensors and can run CONTINUOUSLY (location/movement even when not driving the insured car). Surveys show most non-users decline over privacy, and would only opt in if insurers promised not to sell the data + gave deletion control. grade: fact (the products + the continuous-collection capability).
the_covert_pipeline: The scandal (NYT / Kashmir Hill, Mar 2024): automakers collected detailed driving + precise GEOLOCATION (GM's OnStar 'Smart Driver' as often as every ~3 seconds) and SOLD it to consumer-reporting/data brokers LexisNexis Risk Solutions and Verisk Analytics, which packaged it into risk reports SOLD TO INSURERS — and drivers' premiums rose based on data they never knowingly shared (one driver's premium reportedly jumped ~80% from 603 logged trips). Honda, Hyundai, and Kia were also implicated. GM made ~$20M selling the data and gave consumers NO notice. grade: fact.
the_enforcement: Dated enforcement: (1) FTC v. GM + OnStar — order FINALIZED Jan 2026: a 5-YEAR BAN on sharing geolocation/driver-behavior data with consumer-reporting agencies + ~20-year consent/transparency requirements. (2) California AG — a $12.75M settlement (CCPA data-minimization/purpose-limitation). (3) Texas AG — sued Allstate + its data subsidiary ARITY (Jan 2025) for unlawfully collecting/selling location + movement data from 45M+ trips; Arity embedded SDKs in third-party phone apps (e.g. Life360, GasBuddy) to harvest movement data from people with NO insurance relationship. grade: fact.
the_price_discrimination_risk: The user's first framing, graded carefully: continuous geolocation enables INFERRING behavior/health and pricing on it (e.g., regular visits to a pharmacy could proxy a health condition). This is a documented CAPABILITY/RISK the data pipeline creates — NOT an asserted current insurer practice. Per the corpus's read-words-for-intent rule: insurers SAY UBI is voluntary/discount-only and that they 'don't sell' data, yet the covert automaker sales + Arity's app-SDK harvesting show data flowing WITHOUT consent into risk scoring — so the stated intent and the documented conduct diverge, which is the warranted finding; the specific 'punish you for being sick via pharmacy visits' mechanism is plausible-but-unproven. grade: the price-discrimination CAPABILITY is documented; a specific health-punishment practice is UNVERIFIED (flagged, not adopted).
the_no_alternative_claim: The user's second framing, graded: 'insurers force telematics by offering no alternative' is PARTLY TRUE / PARTLY OVERSTATED. Non-telematics auto insurers still exist (so 'all force it' is unsupported), BUT three structural facts make opting out leaky: (a) the SHARED data-broker layer — LexisNexis/Verisk score drivers ACROSS insurers, so declining one company's app doesn't escape the broker score; (b) the COVERT car pipeline meant your vehicle reported you regardless of any insurance app; (c) the industry trend is telematics-as-default + surveillance-priced books. So the 'collective' effect is real via shared infrastructure + parallel conduct — NOT necessarily coordinated collusion (composition guard: parallel conduct + a shared broker layer != a single 'insurer mind'). grade: 'no alternative at all' unsupported; 'shared-infrastructure coercion/leakiness' documented.
the_honest_reading: Car-insurance telematics is real surveillance pricing, and the worst of it was COVERT: automakers (led by GM/OnStar) sold precise driving + geolocation data to LexisNexis/Verisk, who scored drivers for insurers who raised premiums on data consumers never knowingly shared — until the FTC (Jan 2026), California, and Texas (v. Allstate/Arity) intervened. The user's two concerns grade out as: (1) location-based price discrimination is a documented CAPABILITY the pipeline enables (a specific health-punishment practice is plausible-but-unproven, flagged not asserted), and (2) the 'no alternative' coercion is real not as literal universal mandate but via the SHARED data-broker layer + the covert car pipeline (parallel conduct + shared infrastructure, not proven collusion). Read by read-words-for-intent: insurers' 'voluntary / we don't sell' framing diverges from the documented covert sales. Both sides retained with dates. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: NYT (Kashmir Hill) — automakers quietly shared drivers' data with insurers (Mar 2024)
FTC — order finalized against GM & OnStar over driver data (Jan 2026; 5-yr CRA-sharing ban)
TechCrunch — the FTC's data-sharing order against GM is finally settled (Jan 2026)
Hunton — California AG $12.75M settlement with GM (CCPA)
Texas AG — enforcement action against Allstate + Arity over location-data sales (Jan 2025)
Edmunds — phone apps are selling your driving data to insurers (Arity SDK)

edges.raw_instrument: Arity is Allstate's data/analytics subsidiary — embedded SDKs in third-party phone apps (Life360/GasBuddy) to harvest movement data from non-customers
Drivewise UBI; Texas AG sued Allstate/Arity (Jan 2025, 45M+ trips sold without consent)
always-on phone-SDK movement collection feeding driving scores — even with no insurance relationship
OnStar 'Smart Driver' sold driving + precise geolocation (as often as ~every 3s) to LexisNexis Risk Solutions WITHOUT consumer notice
sold driver-behavior data to Verisk Analytics (~$20M total to both brokers); no consumer consent
packages automaker driving data into 'risk' reports sold to insurers -> premium hikes (one driver +80% from 603 trips)
repackages driver data into insurer risk scores (the shared layer that scores across insurers)
FTC v. GM/OnStar order finalized Jan 2026: 5-YEAR ban on sharing geolocation/driver-behavior data with consumer-reporting agencies + ~20-yr consent/transparency rules
Texas AG sued Allstate + Arity (Jan 2025) for unlawfully collecting/selling location + movement data from 45M+ trips; California AG settled GM for $12.75M (CCPA)
